papergraph.title: Design and Implementation of Scalable Communication Interfaces for Reliable and Stable Real-time Co-Simulation of Power Systems
papergraph.citation: Qi Xiao et al. (2025) Design and Implementation of Scalable Communication Interfaces for Reliable and Stable Real-time Co-Simulation of Power Systems arXiv:2502.07866v1
papergraph.abstract: Co-simulation offers an integrated approach for modeling the large-scale integration of inverter-based resources (IBRs) into transmission and distribution grids. This paper presents a scalable communication interface design and implementation to enable reliable and stable real-time co-simulation of power systems with high IBR penetration. The communication interface is categorized into two types: local and remote. In local scenarios, where subsystems are connected within a single local area network (LAN), low-latency communication facilitates the seamless integration of electromagnetic transient (EMT) and phasor-domain models, enabling efficient interactions with power and energy management algorithms. For remote scenarios, data exchange is achieved via internet-based file sharing or VPN-enabled communication. The performance of both methods is evaluated using OPAL-RT as a real-time simulator, demonstrating scalability, effectiveness, and challenges specific to real-time co-simulation applications. To mitigate instability arising from data resolution mismatches in time-sensitive co-simulations, a real-time data extrapolation method is proposed. This approach significantly enhances stability and reliability, ensuring more accurate simulation outcomes. The implementation code is available on GitHub, providing researchers the tools to replicate and expand upon this work.

papergraph.title: Quantifying and Improving the Accuracy of Electromagnetic Transient-Transient Stability Hybrid Simulation
papergraph.citation: Bin Wang et al. (2026) Quantifying and Improving the Accuracy of Electromagnetic Transient-Transient Stability Hybrid Simulation doi:10.1109/TPWRS.2026.3683988
papergraph.abstract: The increasing penetration of inverter-based resources introduces new dynamic challenges to modern power grids, such as sub- and super-synchronous oscillations and other faster dynamics. These dynamics are typically fast in nature and are difficult to accurately model and analyze using standard transient stability (TS) methods, necessitating the need for electromagnetic transient (EMT) analysis. However, EMT simulations are notoriously slow for large-scale grids due to both equation formulations and computational limitations. To overcome this challenge, EMT-TS hybrid simulation is often used, since it offers a balanced trade-off between accuracy and speed, making it feasible to perform EMT analysis on large systems. One open question about EMT-TS hybrid simulation is the accuracy of the EMT-TS boundary or interface. This paper introduces an error index to quantify EMT-TS hybrid interface errors, identifies conditions where the hybrid simulation approach may become inaccurate, and suggests EMT region expansions to improve the simulation accuracy. Additionally, a three-sequence hybrid interface model is proposed to mitigate inaccuracies caused by unbalanced conditions.

papergraph.title: Digital Twins for Intelligent Intersections: A Literature Review
papergraph.citation: Alben Rome Bagabaldo et al. (2025) Digital Twins for Intelligent Intersections: A Literature Review arXiv:2510.05374v1
papergraph.abstract: Intelligent intersections play a pivotal role in urban mobility, demanding innovative solutions such as digital twins to enhance safety and efficiency. This literature review investigates the integration and application of digital twins for intelligent intersections, a critical area within smart urban traffic systems. The review systematically categorizes existing research into five key thematic areas: (i) Digital Twin Architectures and Frameworks; (ii) Data Processing and Simulation Techniques; (iii) Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning for Adaptive Traffic Control; (iv) Safety and Protection of Vulnerable Road Users; and (v) Scaling from Localized Intersections to Citywide Traffic Networks. Each theme is explored comprehensively, highlighting significant advancements, current challenges, and critical insights. The findings reveal that multi-layered digital twin architectures incorporating real-time data fusion and AI-driven decision-making enhances traffic efficiency and safety. Advanced simulation techniques combined with sophisticated AI/ML algorithms demonstrate notable improvements in real-time responsiveness and predictive accuracy for traffic management. Additionally, the integration of digital twins has shown substantial promise in safeguarding vulnerable road users through proactive and adaptive safety strategies. Despite these advancements, key challenges persist, including interoperability of diverse data sources, scalability of digital twins for extensive traffic networks, and managing uncertainty within dynamic urban environments. Addressing these challenges will be essential for the future development and deployment of intelligent, adaptive, and sustainable intersection management systems.

papergraph.title: Physics-Informed Neural Networks for the Numerical Modeling of Steady-State and Transient Electromagnetic Problems with Discontinuous Media
papergraph.citation: Michel Nohra et al. (2024) Physics-Informed Neural Networks for the Numerical Modeling of Steady-State and Transient Electromagnetic Problems with Discontinuous Media arXiv:2406.04380v1
papergraph.abstract: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) have emerged as a promising numerical method based on deep learning for modeling boundary value problems, showcasing promising results in various fields. In this work, we use PINNs to discretize three-dimensional electromagnetic, parametric problems, with material discontinuities, covering both static and transient regimes. By replacing the discontinuous material properties with a continuous approximation, we eliminate the need to directly enforce interface conditions. Using the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) analysis, we show that using the first-order formulation of Maxwell's equations is more suitable for interface problems. We introduce a PINN-based decomposition on overlapping domains to enhance the convergence rate of the PINN.
papergraph.scenario: three-dimensional electromagnetic problems with material discontinuities
papergraph.contribution: Demonstrating that first-order Maxwell formulation is more suitable for interface problems via NTK analysis, and introducing a PINN-based overlapping domain decomposition to improve convergence rate

papergraph.title: Background-free Tracking of Ultrafast Hole and Electron Dynamics with XUV Transient Grating Spectroscopy
papergraph.citation: Vincent Eggers et al. (2026) Background-free Tracking of Ultrafast Hole and Electron Dynamics with XUV Transient Grating Spectroscopy doi:10.1021/acsphotonics.6c00152
papergraph.abstract: Extreme ultraviolet (XUV) transient absorption (TA) and transient reflectivity (TR) spectroscopies enable element-specific insights into attosecond-timescale processes in solids. XUV transient grating spectroscopy (TGS) is an emerging tool that combines the advantages of both absorption and reflectivity while offering intrinsically background-free detection. Here, we implement XUV-TGS by generating a transient grating in germanium solid using two few-cycle near-infrared pulses and probing it with an attosecond XUV pulse, produced via tabletop high-harmonic generation. The spectrally resolved, diffracted XUV pulses directly visualize the separate ultrashort decay times of both photoexcited electrons and holes, without the need for iterative deconvolution. By combining XUV-TA and -TG spectroscopy, we extract the evolution of the complex refractive index, ñ, without the need for Kramers-Kronig reconstruction, as required in XUV-TR, allowing us to extract the roots of the induced optical response. We find reflectivity changes of up to 34% via the real part of ñ, whereas changes in the imaginary part only result in a variation in reflectivity of around 0.5%.

papergraph.title: Initialization Process of a Power System Transient Simulation Scheme for Stability Studies
papergraph.citation: Sheng Lei et al. (2020) Initialization Process of a Power System Transient Simulation Scheme for Stability Studies arXiv:2008.13059v1
papergraph.abstract: The initialization process of a novel power system transient simulation scheme for stability studies is put forward, by further developing a "time-domain harmonic power-flow algorithm". The initialization process is formulated as an algebraic problem to ensure that the power system under study is in steady state and operated at a specified operating point, at the beginning of a transient simulation run. The algebraic problem is then solved efficiently by a preconditioned finite difference Newton-GMRES method. Case studies verify the validity and efficiency of the initialization process. The proposed initialization process is general-purpose and can be applied to other power system transient simulation schemes.

papergraph.title: A Comprehensive Review on Power System Risk-Based Transient Stability
papergraph.citation: Umair Shahzad (2022) A Comprehensive Review on Power System Risk-Based Transient Stability arXiv:2206.05113v1
papergraph.abstract: Power systems are getting more complex than ever and are consequently operating close to their limit of stability. Moreover, with the increasing demand of renewable wind generation, and the requirement to maintain a secure power system, the importance of transient stability cannot be overestimated. Considering its significance in power system security, it is important to suggest a different methodology for enhancing the transient stability, considering uncertainties. Current deterministic industry practices of transient stability assessment ignore the probabilistic nature of variables (fault type, fault location, fault clearing time, etc.). These approaches typically provide a cautious principle and can result in high-priced expansion projects or operational limits. With the increasing system uncertainties and widespread electricity market deregulation, there is a strong inevitability to incorporate risk in the traditional transient stability analysis. Accurate assessment of transient stability in a modern power network is becoming a strict requirement both in planning and in real-time operation, due to the increasingly intricate dynamics of a power system. Further, increasing sources of uncertainty in forecast state and in the reaction to faults highly implies the implementation of risk-based approach in assessing transient stability. Thus, this paper aims to provide a comprehensive review of risk-based transient stability in power networks and the accompanying research. It is believed that this review can be an inception for researchers in the domain of power system planning and security.

papergraph.title: Towards an Assisted Simulation Planning for Co-Simulation of Cyber-Physical Energy Systems
papergraph.citation: Jan Sören Schwarz et al. (2024) Towards an Assisted Simulation Planning for Co-Simulation of Cyber-Physical Energy Systems doi:10.1109/MSCPES.2019.8738788
papergraph.abstract: Increasing complexity in the power system and the transformation towards a smart grid lead to the necessity of new tools and methods for the development and testing of new technologies. One testing method is co-simulation, which allows coupling simulation components from different domains to test their interaction. Because the manual configuration of complex large-scale co-simulation scenarios can be error-prone, we propose an approach for assisting the user in the development of co-simulation scenarios. Our approach uses an information model, a component catalog implemented in a Semantic Media Wiki, and Semantic Web technologies to assist the high-level modeling of co-simulation scenarios, recommend suitable simulation components, and validate co-simulation scenarios. This assistance aims to improve the usability of co-simulation in the development of interdisciplinary, large-scale scenarios.

papergraph.title: FMI-Based Distributed Co-Simulation with Enhanced Security and Intellectual Property Safeguards
papergraph.citation: Santiago Gil et al. (2025) FMI-Based Distributed Co-Simulation with Enhanced Security and Intellectual Property Safeguards arXiv:2510.20403v1
papergraph.abstract: Distributed co-simulation plays a key role in enabling collaborative modeling and simulation by different stakeholders while protecting their Intellectual Property (IP). Although IP protection is provided implicitly by co-simulation, there is no consensus in the guidelines to conduct distributed co-simulation of continuous-time or hybrid systems with no exposure to potential hacking attacks. We propose an approach for distributed co-simulation on top of UniFMU with enhanced cybersecurity and IP protection mechanisms, ensuring that the connection is initiated by the client and the models and binaries live on trusted platforms. We showcase the functionality of this approach using two co-simulation demos in four different network settings and analyze the trade-off between IP-protected distribution and performance efficiency in these settings.

papergraph.title: Power System Transient Modeling and Simulation using Integrated Circuit
papergraph.citation: Xiang Zhang et al. (2021) Power System Transient Modeling and Simulation using Integrated Circuit arXiv:2106.03254v1
papergraph.abstract: Transient stability analysis (TSA) plays an important role in power system analysis to investigate the stability of power system. Traditionally, transient stability analysis methods have been developed using time domain simulation by means of numerical integration method. In this paper, a new approach is proposed to model power systems as an integrated circuit and simulate the power system dynamic behavior by integrated circuit simulator. The proposed method modeled power grid, generator, governor, and exciter with high fidelity. The power system dynamic simulation accuracy and efficiency of the proposed approach are verified and demonstrated by case study on an IEEE standard system.
papergraph.contribution: A new approach to model power systems as an integrated circuit and simulate dynamic behavior using an integrated circuit simulator, with high-fidelity modeling of power grid, generator, governor, and exciter.

papergraph.title: Using Terminal Circuit for Power System Electromagnetic Transient Simulation
papergraph.citation: Yijing Liu et al. (2021) Using Terminal Circuit for Power System Electromagnetic Transient Simulation arXiv:2107.00540v1
papergraph.abstract: The modern power system is evolving with increasing penetration of power electronics introducing complicated electromagnetic phenomenon. Electromagnetic transient (EMT) simulation is essential to understand power system behavior under disturbance which however is one of the most sophisticated and time-consuming applications in power system. To improve the electromagnetic transient simulation efficiency while keeping the simulation accuracy, this paper proposes to model and simulate power system electromagnetic transients by very large-scale integrated circuit (VLSI) as a preliminary exploration to eventually represent power system by VLSI circuit chip avoiding numerical calculation. To proof the concept, a simple 5 bus system is modeled and simulated to verify the feasibility of the proposed approach.

papergraph.title: Real-time frequency based reduced order modeling of large power grid
papergraph.citation: Abilash Thakallapelli et al. (2019) Real-time frequency based reduced order modeling of large power grid doi:10.1109/PESGM.2016.7741877
papergraph.abstract: Large power systems are complex and real-time modeling of the grid for electromagnetic simulation (EMT) studies is impractical. In general, there are methods that reduce large power system into an equivalent network that requires less computational resource, while preserving electromechanical (low frequency) and high frequency behavior of the original system. This can be achieved by modeling the area not of interest (external area) as a combination of Transient Stability Analysis (TSA) type phasor model equivalent and Frequency Dependent Network Equivalent (FDNE). TSA retains electromechanical behavior, whereas FDNE retains high frequency behavior of the original power system. To this effect, this paper introduces a method of developing FDNE based on an online recursive least squares (RLS) identification algorithm in z-domain, and modeling of reduced power systems as FDNE and as a combination of TSA and FDNE using real-time digital simulators.

papergraph.title: Context in object detection: a systematic literature review
papergraph.citation: Mahtab Jamali et al. (2025) Context in object detection: a systematic literature review doi:10.1007/s10462-025-11186-x
papergraph.abstract: Context is an important factor in computer vision as it offers valuable information to clarify and analyze visual data. Utilizing the contextual information inherent in an image or a video can improve the precision and effectiveness of object detectors. For example, where recognizing an isolated object might be challenging, context information can improve comprehension of the scene. This study explores the impact of various context-based approaches to object detection. Initially, we investigate the role of context in object detection and survey it from several perspectives. We then review and discuss the most recent context-based object detection approaches and compare them. Finally, we conclude by addressing research questions and identifying gaps for further studies. More than 265 publications are included in this survey, covering different aspects of context in different categories of object detection, including general object detection, video object detection, small object detection, camouflaged object detection, zero-shot, one-shot, and few-shot object detection. This literature review presents a comprehensive overview of the latest advancements in context-based object detection, providing valuable contributions such as a thorough understanding of contextual information and effective methods for integrating various context types into object detection, thus benefiting researchers.

papergraph.title: Deform360: A Massive Multi-view Visuotactile Dataset for Deformable World Models
papergraph.citation: Hongyu Li et al. (2026) Deform360: A Massive Multi-view Visuotactile Dataset for Deformable World Models arXiv:2607.05390v1
papergraph.abstract: Predicting object dynamics (i.e., world modeling) is a fundamental challenge for robotic manipulation, and modeling deformable objects presents a particularly difficult case due to their high-dimensional state spaces and complex material properties. While current world models approach this through two distinct paradigms: learning the dynamics over the 2D pixel space or more explicit 3D geometric space. A systematic understanding of their relative strengths and limitations remains elusive due to the lack of diverse, large-scale real-world data. To address this, we present Deform360, a large-scale visuotactile dataset featuring 198 daily-life objects, 1,980 interaction sequences, and over 215 hours of observations from 41 surround-view cameras and bimanual tactile grippers to capture both global motion and contact-induced local deformations. Leveraging a novel markerless visuotactile 3D tracking pipeline to extract dense geometry and motion, we systematically evaluate current state-of-the-art world models, comparing 2D video models against 3D particle models. Finally, we provide a preliminary demonstration indicating the real-world applicability of our dataset by performing robot planning tasks on deformable objects. Our analysis reveals key insights into the trade-offs between structural priors and scalability, providing a solid benchmark for future research in generalizable deformable object-centric world modeling. Project website: https://deform360.lhy.xyz

papergraph.title: From Fixed to Free Cameras: Calibration-Free View-Robust Vision-Language-Action Model
papergraph.citation: Wenhao Li et al. (2026) From Fixed to Free Cameras: Calibration-Free View-Robust Vision-Language-Action Model arXiv:2607.05396v1
papergraph.abstract: Real-world robot deployment rarely maintains the training-stage camera setup, where cameras often experience repositioning or remounting depending on actual scenarios. Existing view-robust Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies tolerate such camera variations only when the camera extrinsics are explicitly provided, making them fragile and hard to use especially when view robustness is critical. We argue that the policy should not be told where the camera is, but rather figure it out by itself. To this end, we introduce Camera-Centric VLA (CamVLA), a new VLA model that decouples manipulation controls from camera geometry by predicting (i) a camera-centric end-effector action expressed in the local camera frame, and (ii) a 6-DoF hand-eye matrix relating cameras to the robot base. A deterministic geometric transformation composes the two predictions into a robot base-frame action. This disentangles how I should move in pose-independent camera-centric action generation from where I am looking from in camera-perspective geometric grounding. The resulting policy is calibration-free, depth-free, and single-view, requiring only a single monocular RGB image as the visual observation and task instruction at deployment. Evaluations in both simulation and real-world robot data show that CamVLA consistently improves success rates across diverse unseen viewpoints. Project page: https://alibaba-damo-academy.github.io/CamVLA/.

keynotes.title: Design and Implementation of Scalable Communication Interfaces for Reliable and Stable Real-time Co-Simulation of Power Systems

keynote.title: Design and Implementation of Scalable Communication Interfaces for Reliable and Stable Real-time Co-Simulation of Power Systems
keynote.core_problem: Enabling reliable and stable real-time co-simulation of power systems with high inverter-based resource (IBR) penetration, particularly under communication latency, time-resolution mismatches, and distributed (local vs. remote) subsystem coupling.
contributions: A scalable communication interface design categorized into local (LAN-based) and remote (internet/VPN-based) types
A real-time data extrapolation method to mitigate instability from time-resolution mismatches
Implementation and evaluation using OPAL-RT as a real-time simulator
Open-source implementation available on GitHub
methods: Local communication via low-latency LAN for coupling EMT and phasor-domain models
Remote communication via internet-based file sharing or VPN-enabled channels
Real-time data extrapolation to resolve time-resolution mismatches
experiments_or_evidence: Performance evaluation using OPAL-RT real-time simulator
Demonstration of scalability, effectiveness, and identification of challenges specific to real-time co-simulation
assumptions: Subsystems can be meaningfully partitioned into EMT and phasor domains
LAN infrastructure provides sufficiently low latency for local co-simulation stability
Remote communication channels (e.g., VPN) provide acceptable determinism for non-critical timing paths
limitations: Challenges specific to real-time co-simulation applications (explicitly noted but not enumerated in provided text)

gap_signals.text: Challenges specific to real-time co-simulation applications (explicitly noted but not enumerated in provided text)
Lack of robust real-time communication interfaces supporting both local and remote subsystem coupling at scale
Instability arising from data resolution mismatches in time-sensitive co-simulations

important_claims.claim: Real-time data extrapolation significantly enhances stability and reliability in co-simulation with mismatched time resolutions
important_claims.evidence: The approach is stated to 'significantly enhance stability and reliability, ensuring more accurate simulation outcomes'

important_claims.claim: The proposed communication interface enables seamless integration of EMT and phasor-domain models in local scenarios
important_claims.evidence: Low-latency LAN communication 'facilitates the seamless integration of electromagnetic transient (EMT) and phasor-domain models'

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: High: Provides an open, evaluated architecture and implementation for real-time power system co-simulation—particularly valuable for IBR-integration studies, hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) setups, and distributed co-simulation frameworks requiring local/remote flexibility.

keynote.title: Quantifying and Improving the Accuracy of Electromagnetic Transient-Transient Stability Hybrid Simulation
keynote.core_problem: Accuracy degradation at the EMT-TS hybrid interface, especially under fast dynamics from inverter-based resources and unbalanced conditions.
contributions: An error index to quantify EMT-TS hybrid interface errors
Identification of conditions under which hybrid simulation becomes inaccurate
Proposal of EMT region expansions to improve accuracy
A three-sequence hybrid interface model to mitigate inaccuracies under unbalanced conditions
methods: Error index formulation for EMT-TS boundary quantification
Analysis of interface accuracy conditions
EMT region expansion strategy
Three-sequence hybrid interface modeling

gap_signals.text: One open question about EMT-TS hybrid simulation is the accuracy of the EMT-TS boundary or interface.

important_claims.evidence: Abstract states increasing inverter-based resources introduce fast dynamics (sub-/super-synchronous oscillations) difficult to model accurately with standard TS methods, necessitating EMT—but EMT is too slow, motivating hybrid use; yet 'one open question' is interface accuracy.

important_claims.claim: The proposed three-sequence hybrid interface model mitigates inaccuracies caused by unbalanced conditions.
important_claims.evidence: Paper text explicitly states: 'a three-sequence hybrid interface model is proposed to mitigate inaccuracies caused by unbalanced conditions.'

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a reusable error metric and interface modeling approach for hybrid power system simulation; enables systematic diagnosis and improvement of co-simulation boundaries in multi-timescale energy system modeling.

contributions: Systematic categorization of existing research into five thematic areas: (i) Digital Twin Architectures and Frameworks; (ii) Data Processing and Simulation Techniques; (iii) AI/ML for Adaptive Traffic Control; (iv) Safety and Protection of Vulnerable Road Users; (v) Scaling from Localized Intersecti...[truncated]

keynotes.title: Physics-Informed Neural Networks for the Numerical Modeling of Steady-State and Transient Electromagnetic Problems with Discontinuous Media

keynote.title: Physics-Informed Neural Networks for the Numerical Modeling of Steady-State and Transient Electromagnetic Problems with Discontinuous Media
keynote.core_problem: Numerical modeling of 3D electromagnetic problems with material discontinuities in both steady-state and transient regimes, where traditional PINNs struggle with interface conditions.
contributions: Use of PINNs for 3D parametric electromagnetic problems with material discontinuities across static and transient regimes
Replacement of discontinuous material properties with continuous approximations to avoid explicit enforcement of interface conditions
Demonstration via Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) analysis that first-order Maxwell formulation is more suitable for interface problems
Introduction of a PINN-based overlapping domain decomposition method to enhance convergence rate
methods: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs)
Continuous approximation of discontinuous material properties
Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) analysis
First-order formulation of Maxwell's equations
Overlapping domain decomposition for PINNs
experiments_or_evidence: NTK analysis supporting suitability of first-order Maxwell formulation for interface problems
Empirical demonstration of improved convergence rate using overlapping domain decomposition

keynotes.title: Background-free Tracking of Ultrafast Hole and Electron Dynamics with XUV Transient Grating Spectroscopy

keynote.title: Background-free Tracking of Ultrafast Hole and Electron Dynamics with XUV Transient Grating Spectroscopy
keynote.core_problem: Separating and resolving ultrafast, element-specific electron and hole dynamics in solids on attosecond timescales without background contamination or iterative deconvolution artifacts.
contributions: First implementation of XUV transient grating spectroscopy (TGS) in a solid (germanium)
Background-free, spectrally resolved detection of distinct electron and hole decay times
Direct extraction of the complex refractive index ñ without Kramers-Kronig reconstruction by combining XUV-TA and XUV-TG
Quantification of differential contributions of real and imaginary parts of ñ to reflectivity changes (34% vs. ~0.5%)
methods: XUV transient grating spectroscopy (TGS) using two few-cycle near-infrared pulses to generate a transient grating in germanium
Attosecond XUV probe pulse generated via tabletop high-harmonic generation
Spectrally resolved detection of diffracted XUV signal
Combined analysis of XUV transient absorption (TA) and XUV transient grating (TG) data to extract complex refractive index ñ
experiments_or_evidence: Measurement of separate ultrashort decay times for photoexcited electrons and holes in germanium
Observation of up to 34% reflectivity change attributed to real part of ñ
Observation of ~0.5% reflectivity change attributed to imaginary part of ñ
Demonstration of background-free detection without iterative deconvolution
assumptions: The transient grating formed by NIR pulses is sufficiently coherent and stable for XUV diffraction
High-harmonic generation produces an adequate attosecond XUV pulse for time-resolved diffraction
Germanium’s band structure and optical response permit clean separation of electron/hole signatures in the XUV-TG signal

gap_signals.text: Prior XUV techniques (TA, TR) required either iterative deconvolution (TA) or Kramers-Kronig reconstruction (TR); this work bypasses both.

keynote.title: From Fixed to Free Cameras: Calibration-Free View-Robust Vision-Language-Action Model
keynote.core_problem: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies require explicit camera extrinsics to handle camera repositioning, making them fragile and impractical for real-world robot deployment where camera setup changes unpredictably.
contributions: Introduces Camera-Centric VLA (CamVLA), a calibration-free, depth-free, single-view VLA model.

Proposes decoupling manipulation control from camera geometry via two predictions: (i) camera-centric end-effector action in local camera frame, and (ii) 6-DoF hand-eye matrix relating camera to robot base.
Uses deterministic geometric transformation to compose predictions into robot base-frame action, enabling self-grounding of camera geometry without explicit extrinsics.
methods: Camera-centric action prediction in local camera frame.
Prediction of 6-DoF hand-eye transformation matrix (camera-to-robot-base).
Deterministic geometric composition of camera-centric action and hand-eye matrix to yield base-frame action.
experiments_or_evidence: Evaluations in both simulation and real-world robot data.
Consistent improvement in success rates across diverse unseen viewpoints.
assumptions: The hand-eye relationship can be modeled as a rigid 6-DoF transformation.
A single monocular RGB image and task instruction are sufficient for grounding camera geometry and action prediction.

gap_signals.text: Prior methods do not decouple action generation from camera geometry; CamVLA explicitly disentangles pose-independent action prediction from camera-perspective grounding.

Co-simulation offers an integrated approach for modeling the large-scale integration of inverter-based resources (IBRs) into transmission and distribution grids.

Abstract: The increasing penetration of inverter-based resources introduces new dynamic challenges to modern power grids, such as sub- and super-synchronous oscillations and other faster dynamics. These dynamics are typically fast in nature and are difficult to accurately model and analyze using sta...[truncated]
Full text excerpt:

These dynamics are typically fast in nature and are difficult to accurately model and analyze using standard transient stability (TS) methods, necessitating the need for electromagnetic transient (EMT) analysis.
However, EMT simulations are notoriously slow for large-scale grids due to both equation formulations and computational limitations.
To overcome this challenge, EMT-TS hybrid simulation is often used, since it offers a balanced trade-off between accuracy and speed, making it feasible to perform EMT analysis on large systems.
One open question about EMT-TS hybrid simulation is the accuracy of the EMT-TS boundary or interface.

This paper introduces an error index to quantify EMT-TS hybrid interface errors, identifies conditions where the hybrid simulation approach may become inaccurate, and suggests EMT region expansions to improve the simulation accuracy.

Full text excerpt:
Considering its significance in power system security, it is important to suggest a different methodology for enhancing the transient stability, considering uncertainties.
These approaches typically provide a cautious principle and can result in high-priced expansion projects or operational limits.
Further, increasing sources of uncertainty in forecast state and in the reaction to faults highly implies the implementation of risk-based approach in assessing transient stability.

references: Jan Sören Schwarz et al. (2024) Towards an Assisted Simulation Planning for Co-Simulation of Cyber-Physical Energy Systems doi:10.1109/MSCPES.2019.8738788

evidence_type_annotations.claim: towards an assisted simulation

An assisted simulation planning approach that supports high-level modeling, component recommendation, and validation of co-simulation scenarios using Semantic Web technologies and an information model.
No explicit limitation extracted.
Full text excerpt:
Increasing complexity in the power system and the transformation towards a smart grid lead to the necessity of new tools and methods for the development and testing of new technologies.
One testing method is co-simulation, which allows coupling simulation components from different domains to test their interaction.
Because the manual configuration of complex large-scale co-simulation scenarios can be error-prone, we propose an approach for assisting the user in the development of co-simulation scenarios.

Our approach uses an information model, a component catalog implemented in a Semantic Media Wiki, and Semantic Web technologies to assist the high-level modeling of co-simulation scenarios, recommend suitable simulation components, and validate co-simulation scenarios.
This assistance aims to improve the usability of co-simulation in the development of interdisciplinary, large-scale scenarios.

Full text excerpt: An assisted simulation planning approach that supports high-level modeling, component recommendation, and validation of co-simulation scenarios using Semantic Web technologies and an information model.

We propose an approach for distributed co-simulation on top of UniFMU with enhanced cybersecurity and IP protection mechanisms, ensuring that the connection is initiated by the client and the models and binaries live on trusted platforms.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: integrated circuit modeling and simulation

A new approach to model power systems as an integrated circuit and simulate dynamic behavior using an integrated circuit simulator, with high-fidelity modeling of power grid, generator, governor, and exciter.
No explicit limitation extracted.
Full text excerpt:
Traditionally, transient stability analysis methods have been developed using time domain simulation by means of numerical integration method.
In this paper, a new approach is proposed to model power systems as an integrated circuit and simulate the power system dynamic behavior by integrated circuit simulator.
The proposed method modeled power grid, generator, governor, and exciter with high fidelity.
The power system dynamic simulation accuracy and efficiency of the proposed approach are verified and demonstrated by case study on an IEEE standard system.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: scalable communication interface design | real-time data extrapolation method
A scalable communication interface design and a real-time data extrapolation method that enhance stability and reliability in real-time co-simulation of power systems with high IBR penetration.
Challenges specific to real-time co-simulation applications.
Full text excerpt:
Co-simulation offers an integrated approach for modeling the large-scale integration of inverter-based resources (IBRs) into transmission and distribution grids.

In local scenarios, where subsystems are connected within a single local area network (LAN), low-latency communication facilitates the seamless integration of electromagnetic transient (EMT) and phasor-domain models, enabling efficient interactions with power and energy management algorithms.
The performance of both methods is evaluated using OPAL-RT as a real-time simulator, demonstrating scalability, effectiveness, and challenges specific to real-time co-simulation applications.
To mitigate instability arising from data resolution mismatches in time-sensitive co-simulations, a real-time data extrapolation method is proposed.
This approach significantly enhances stability and reliability, ensuring more accurate simulation outcomes.

Full text excerpt: A scalable communication interface design and a real-time data extrapolation method that enhance stability and reliability in real-time co-simulation of power systems with high IBR penetration.

Abstract: The increasing penetration of inverter-based resources introduces new dynamic challenges to modern power grids, such as sub- and super-synchronous oscillations and other faster dynamics. These dynamics are typically fast in nature and are difficult to accurately model and analyze using sta...[truncated]
Full text excerpt:

These dynamics are typically fast in nature and are difficult to accurately model and analyze using standard transient stability (TS) methods, necessitating the need for electromagnetic transient (EMT) analysis.
However, EMT simulations are notoriously slow for large-scale grids due to both equation formulations and computational limitations.
To overcome this challenge, EMT-TS hybrid simulation is often used, since it offers a balanced trade-off between accuracy and speed, making it feasible to perform EMT analysis on large systems.
One open question about EMT-TS hybrid simulation is the accuracy of the EMT-TS boundary or interface.

This paper introduces an error index to quantify EMT-TS hybrid interface errors, identifies conditions where the hybrid simulation approach may become inaccurate, and suggests EMT region expansions to improve the simulation accuracy.

references: Jan Sören Schwarz et al. (2024) Towards an Assisted Simulation Planning for Co-Simulation of Cyber-Physical Energy Systems doi:10.1109/MSCPES.2019.8738788

evidence_type_annotations.claim: towards an assisted simulation

An assisted simulation planning approach that supports high-level modeling, component recommendation, and validation of co-simulation scenarios using Semantic Web technologies and an information model.
No explicit limitation extracted.
Full text excerpt:
Increasing complexity in the power system and the transformation towards a smart grid lead to the necessity of new tools and methods for the development and testing of new technologies.
One testing method is co-simulation, which allows coupling simulation components from different domains to test their interaction.
Because the manual configuration of complex large-scale co-simulation scenarios can be error-prone, we propose an approach for assisting the user in the development of co-simulation scenarios.

Our approach uses an information model, a component catalog implemented in a Semantic Media Wiki, and Semantic Web technologies to assist the high-level modeling of co-simulation scenarios, recommend suitable simulation components, and validate co-simulation scenarios.
This assistance aims to improve the usability of co-simulation in the development of interdisciplinary, large-scale scenarios.

Full text excerpt: An assisted simulation planning approach that supports high-level modeling, component recommendation, and validation of co-simulation scenarios using Semantic Web technologies and an information model.

Traditionally, transient stability analysis methods have been developed using time domain simulation by means of numerical integration method.
In this paper, a new approach is proposed to model power systems as an integrated circuit and simulate the power system dynamic behavior by integrated circuit simulator.
The proposed method modeled power grid, generator, governor, and exciter with high fidelity.
The power system dynamic simulation accuracy and efficiency of the proposed approach are verified and demonstrated by case study on an IEEE standard system.
